topics.title: Legislations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legislations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Environmental Legislations — Definition

In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, *Legislations* refer to the body of **national statutes, acts, rules, notifications, and amendments** enacted by the Parliament of India and the competent regulatory authorities (MoEFCC, CPCB, SPCB) that **mandate, regulate, and govern** the process of assessing, mitigating, and managing the environmental consequences of proposed developmental projects and industrial activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Environmental legislation forms the **legal backbone** of the EIA process in India. Without statutory backing, an EIA report is merely an academic document. It is the *law* that gives the EIA report its enforceability and penalty-anchored authority.

Formally, under **NEP 2020 and the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework**, legislations in EIA are studied at three hierarchical tiers:

| Tier | Type of Instrument | Binding Nature | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tier 1 | **Umbrella Acts** (Parliament Laws) | Highest | Environment (Protection) Act, **1986** |
| Tier 2 | **Sub-sector Acts & Rules** | Statutory | Water Act **1974**, Air Act **1981** |
| Tier 3 | **Notifications & Amendments** | Administrative | EIA Notification **2006**, CRZ Notification **2019** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rule Book" of Development

Imagine a city where thousands of vehicles run every day. Without traffic signals, speed limits, helmet laws, and pollution-under-control (PUC) certificates, the city would collapse into chaos. Environmental legislations play **exactly the same role** for industries and developmental projects:

- **Vehicles** → Industrial projects, mining, construction
- **Driver's License (Eligibility)** → **Environmental Clearance (EC)** before starting a project
- **Traffic Police** → Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) & State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)
- **PUC Certificate** → Consent to Establish (CTE) & Consent to Operate (CTO)
- **Court for Violations** → National Green Tribunal (NGT)
- **Supreme Rule Book** → **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**

> [!NOTE]
> Just as traffic rules exist *before* accidents happen (preventive), EIA legislations are **ex-ante** (before-the-fact) tools — they stop damage *before* it occurs, rather than punishing it afterwards.

---

## 1.3 Why Legislations Are Indispensable for EIA

For an EIA to be legally binding, three statutory pillars are mandatory:

1. **Legal Mandate** — A law must *require* the EIA to be conducted (e.g., EIA Notification 2006).
2. **Institutional Authority** — A designated agency must *review and approve* the EIA report (e.g., Expert Appraisal Committee — EAC, State Environment Impact Assessment Authority — SEIAA).
3. **Penalty & Redressal Mechanism** — Violations must attract *enforceable consequences* (e.g., NGT Act 2010, Section 15 of EP Act 1986).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trivia:** If asked *"Which is the umbrella legislation for EIA in India?"*, the only correct answer is the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. All other acts are either *predecessors* (Water/Air Acts) or *specialized extensions*.

---

## 1.4 Important Acronyms for KTU Examination

| Acronym | Full Form |
| :--- | :--- |
| **MoEFCC** | Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change |
| **CPCB** | Central Pollution Control Board |
| **SPCB** | State Pollution Control Board |
| **EAC** | Expert Appraisal Committee |
| **SEAC** | State Expert Appraisal Committee |
| **SEIAA** | State Environment Impact Assessment Authority |
| **NGT** | National Green Tribunal |
| **EC** | Environmental Clearance |
| **CTE** | Consent to Establish |
| **CTO** | Consent to Operate |
| **CRZ** | Coastal Regulation Zone |
| **CZA** | Central Zoo Authority |
| **NBA** | National Biodiversity Authority |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Legislative Pyramid of Indian EIA

The Indian environmental legal framework follows a **strict hierarchical structure** — much like a pyramid. At the apex sits the constitutional mandate, followed by umbrella acts, sectoral acts, rules, and finally notifications.

### Tier 1 — Constitutional Foundation

- **Article 48A** of the Indian Constitution (inserted by the **42nd Amendment, 1976**) — *Directive Principles of State Policy*: The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country.
- **Article 51A(g)** — *Fundamental Duties*: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures.

### Tier 2 — The Umbrella Act

#### Environment (Protection) Act, **1986** (EPA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important Act** for KTU examinations on EIA Legislations.

- **Enacted under:** Article 253 of the Constitution (following the Stockholm Conference, 1972 and the Bhopal Gas Tragedy, 1984).
- **Purpose:** An umbrella legislation to provide for the protection and improvement of environment and for matters connected therewith.
- **Salient Features:**
  - Empowered the **Central Government** to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment.
  - Authorized the Central Government to **prescribe standards** for emission/effluent discharge.
  - Established the **authority to close, prohibit, or regulate** any industry causing pollution (Section 5).
  - Provided for **penalties** under Section 15 — imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both.
  - **Daily default fine:** Additional fine of up to **₹5,000** per day for continuing violations.
- **Significance for EIA:** EPA 1986 is the **legal parent** under which the EIA Notification 2006 was issued. Without EPA, the EIA process would have no statutory force.

### Tier 3 — Sectoral / Predecessor Acts

#### Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1974**

- **First comprehensive environmental statute** of independent India.
- Created the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** at the Centre and **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** at the State level.
- Established the **consent mechanism** — industries must obtain *Consent to Establish (CTE)* before construction and *Consent to Operate (CTO)* before commissioning.
- **Amended in 1988** to align with EPA 1986.

#### Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1981**

- An extension of the Water Act to the **atmospheric environment**.
- Empowers SPCBs to set **ambient air quality standards** and **emission standards** for industries.
- The **1987 amendment** made it fully aligned with EPA 1986.

### Tier 4 — Specialized Acts

| Act / Rule | Year | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Wildlife Protection Act | **1972** | Protects wild animals, birds, and plants; creates Protected Areas (Sanctuaries, National Parks). |
| Forest Conservation Act | **1980** | Restricts diversion of forest land for non-forest use; mandates FC (Forest Clearance). |
| Indian Forest Act | **1927** | Older colonial-era law — regulates forest transit, timber, and grazing. |
| Public Liability Insurance Act | **1991** | Mandatory insurance for industries handling hazardous substances to provide immediate relief to victims. |
| National Environment Tribunal Act | **1995** | Established NGT predecessor for speedy redressal (later replaced by NGT Act 2010). |
| Biological Diversity Act | **2002** | Conservation of biological diversity, sustainable use, and equitable sharing of benefits (implements CBD). |
| National Green Tribunal Act | **2010** | Established **NGT** for effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection. |
| Wildlife Protection Amendment Act | **2022** | Strengthens wildlife conservation; includes management plans for protected areas. |

### Tier 5 — Notifications & Rules

| Notification / Rule | Year | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| EIA Notification | **1994** | First-ever mandatory EIA requirement for specified projects. |
| EIA Notification (Revised) | **2006** | Comprehensive framework — categorizes projects into **Category A** (Central) and **Category B** (State). |
| CRZ Notification | **2011 / 2019** | Regulates activities along India's coastline (up to 500 m from HTL). |
| Solid Waste Management Rules | **2016** | Replaces Municipal Solid Waste Rules 2000. |
| Construction & Demolition Waste Rules | **2016** | Management of C&D waste. |
| Hazardous Waste Management Rules | **2016** | Replaces 1989 rules; aligns with Basel Convention. |
| Plastic Waste Management Rules | **2016** | Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). |
| E-Waste Management Rules | **2016 / 2022** | Management of electronic waste. |
| EIA Notification Amendment | **2020** | Draft EIA 2020 — controversial for diluting public consultation (postponed). |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — EIA Legislations

| Legislation | Year | Key Provision | EIA Relevance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Article 48A** | 1976 | Constitutional duty on State | Provides constitutional basis |
| **Article 51A(g)** | 1976 | Fundamental duty on citizen | Public participation in EIA |
| Water Act | **1974** | CPCB/SPCB; Consent mechanism | CTE & CTO |
| Wildlife Protection Act | **1972** | Protected areas | Critical habitat assessment |
| Forest Conservation Act | **1980** | FC clearance | Forest diversion in EIA |
| Air Act | **1981** | Emission standards | Air quality modelling |
| **EPA (Umbrella Act)** | **1986** | Section 5: regulatory power | **Legal basis for EIA Notification** |
| Public Liability Insurance Act | **1991** | Hazardous industry insurance | Risk assessment in EIA |
| Biodiversity Act | **2002** | NBA, SBB | Biodiversity impact |
| **EIA Notification** | **2006** | Categories A & B | **Mandatory EIA framework** |
| NGT Act | **2010** | Tribunal for environmental disputes | Post-EIA enforcement |
| CRZ Notification | **2011/2019** | Coastal zone | EIA for coastal projects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "WAFEN 1972-2010":**
> **W**ildlife (1972), **A**ir (1981), **F**orest (1980), **E**PA (1986), **N**GT (2010).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Projects

For a B.Tech student, understanding EIA legislations is **not optional** — it directly affects:

1. **Site Selection** — Whether a site falls in CRZ, forest, or ecologically sensitive area (ESA) decides if the project is even permissible.
2. **Cost Estimation** — Environmental clearance timelines (typically 60–120 days) affect project schedules.
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 mandates insurance — a direct financial liability.
4. **Career Relevance** — Environmental engineers, civil engineers, and project managers must comply with these statutes daily.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Breakdown — EIA Notification 2006 (The Operational Backbone)

The EIA Notification 2006 is the **single most operationally critical document** for EIA in India. Let us analytically derive its structure.

### Step 1: Project Categorization Logic

Under the EIA Notification 2006 (as amended), all projects are classified into:

$$
C_{total} = \{ C_A \} \cup \{ C_{B1} \} \cup \{ C_{B2} \}
$$

Where:

- $C_A$ — **Category A** projects: Appraised by the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** at the Central level (MoEFCC). These are large-scale or potentially high-impact projects. They **always require prior Environmental Clearance (EC)**.

- $C_{B1}$ — **Category B1** projects: Appraised by the **State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)** and approved by **SEIAA**. These require EIA reports *and* public consultation.

- $C_{B2}$ — **Category B2** projects: Do **not require an EIA report** but require a *Form 1 / Form 1A* (application form with basic information) and approval from SEIAA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic Check:** A project shifts from Category B to Category A if it is located within **10 km** of an **Ecologically Sensitive Area (ESA)** (e.g., National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, UNESCO World Heritage Site).

### Step 2: The Clearance Decision Tree

The decision logic for Environmental Clearance can be represented as:

$$
EC = \begin{cases}
\text{Mandatory (EAC)} & \text{if } P \in C_A \\
\text{Mandatory (SEAC+SEIAA)} & \text{if } P \in C_{B1} \\
\text{Not Required (Form 1)} & \text{if } P \in C_{B2} \\
\text{Exempted} & \text{if } P \in \text{Schedule (Negative List)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $P$ denotes the proposed project.

### Step 3: The Four-Stage EIA Process (Operational Derivation)

The EIA cycle under Notification 2006 consists of **four sequential stages**:

$$
EIA_{cycle} = S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_3 \rightarrow S_4
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \text{Screening (ToR — Terms of Reference formulation)} \\
S_2 &= \text{Scoping (Identification of key impacts and study area)} \\
S_3 &= \text{Impact Analysis (Baseline data + prediction + mitigation)} \\
S_4 &= \text{Review \& Decision (Public Hearing + Appraisal + EC Grant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **Category A** projects, the **ToR is approved by EAC**, while for **Category B** projects, it is approved by **SEAC**. Public consultation (Public Hearing) is **mandatory** for Category A and Category B1 projects but **exempted** for B2.

### Step 4: Penalty Computation under EPA 1986

For any violation of EPA 1986, Section 15 prescribes:

$$
P_{total} = P_{fixed} + (D_{default} \times F_{daily})
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= \text{Total Penalty} \\
P_{fixed} &= \text{Up to } ₹1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (fixed component)} \\
D_{default} &= \text{Number of days the violation continues} \\
F_{daily} &= \text{Up to } ₹5{,}000 \text{ per day (daily component)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A chemical industry operates without Environmental Clearance for **120 days**. Compute the maximum penalty.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fixed} &= ₹1{,}00{,}000 \\
D_{default} &= 120 \text{ days} \\
F_{daily} &= ₹5{,}000 \text{ per day} \\
P_{additional} &= 120 \times 5{,}000 = ₹6{,}00{,}000 \\
P_{total} &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The total statutory penalty = **₹7,00,000** (plus possible imprisonment up to 5 years under Section 15).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Table — Water Act vs Air Act

For KTU 2-mark comparison questions, the following analytical matrix is high-yield:

| Parameter | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Year of enactment | **1974** | **1981** |
| Environmental medium | Water bodies, streams | Atmospheric air |
| Statutory body created | CPCB & SPCBs | Empowered SPCBs (CPCB already existed) |
| Key mechanism | **Consent (CTE & CTO)** | **Consent (CTE & CTO)** |
| Significant amendment | **1988** (post-EPA 1986) | **1987** (post-EPA 1986) |
| Penalties | Section 24–25 | Section 22–23 |
| Predecessor status | First Indian pollution Act | Extension of Water Act |

---

## 3.3 Categorization of Industries under Water & Air Acts

Industries are categorized based on pollution potential index (PPI). The CPCB classifies them as:

$$
\text{Industry Type} = \begin{cases}
\text{Red} & \text{High Pollution Potential (PPI > 70)} \\
\text{Orange} & \text{Medium Pollution Potential (40 ≤ PPI ≤ 70)} \\
\text{Green} & \text{Low Pollution Potential (PPI < 40)} \\
\text{White} & \text{Non-polluting}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Red category industries** require both **CTE and CTO** and are subject to the most stringent monitoring. Examples: Tanneries, cement plants, distilleries, integrated steel plants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Indian EIA Legislations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["CONSTITUTION OF INDIA"] --> B1["Article 48A and 51A g"]
    B1 --> C1["Environment Protection Act 1986 Umbrella Act"]
    C1 --> D1["Water Act 1974"]
    C1 --> D2["Air Act 1981"]
    C1 --> D3["Wildlife Protection Act 1972"]
    C1 --> D4["Forest Conservation Act 1980"]
    C1 --> D5["Public Liability Insurance Act 1991"]
    C1 --> D6["Biological Diversity Act 2002"]
    D1 --> E1["Consent to Establish CTE"]
    D1 --> E2["Consent to Operate CTO"]
    D2 --> E3["Emission Standards"]
    D3 --> E4["Protected Areas"]
    D4 --> E5["Forest Clearance FC"]
    C1 --> F1["EIA Notification 2006"]
    F1 --> G1["Category A Central Appraisal"]
    F1 --> G2["Category B State Appraisal"]
    G1 --> H1["EAC Expert Appraisal Committee"]
    G2 --> H2["SEAC SEIAA"]
    C1 --> F2["CRZ Notification 2019"]
    C1 --> F3["Hazardous Waste Rules 2016"]
    C1 --> F4["Solid Waste Rules 2016"]
    F1 --> I1["NGT Act 2010 Redressal Forum"]
    I1 --> J1["Civil Court Jurisdiction Barred"]
    I1 --> J2["Penalty and Compensation Orders"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Trace from the top — Constitution → EPA 1986 → Sectoral Acts → Notifications → Operational Clearances → NGT (final adjudicating authority). Every arrow represents a *legal derivation*, not merely a logical connection.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Environmental Clearance Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Project Proposal by Promoter"] --> P2["Screening Category A or B"]
    P2 --> P3{"Category A?"}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4["EAC Appraisal at Centre"]
    P3 -- No --> P5["SEAC then SEIAA at State"]
    P4 --> P6["Public Consultation Hearing"]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7["EIA Report Preparation"]
    P7 --> P8["Review by Appraisal Committee"]
    P8 --> P9{"EC Granted?"}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10["Validity 7 Years for Category A"]
    P9 -- No --> P11["Rejection with Reasons"]
    P10 --> P12["Monitoring Compliance Phase"]
    P12 --> P13["Six Monthly Compliance Reports"]
    P13 --> P14{"Violation Detected?"}
    P14 -- Yes --> P15["NGT Adjudication"]
    P14 -- No --> P16["Project Operation Continues"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationship Between Acts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1["Water Act 1974"] --> L1["Created CPCB and SPCB"]
    K2["Air Act 1981"] --> L1
    L1 --> M1["EPA 1986 Strengthened Their Powers"]
    M1 --> N1["EIA Notification 2006 Operational Tool"]
    N1 --> O1["Category A B1 B2 Classification"]
    O1 --> P1["EAC SEAC SEIAA Authorities"]
    P1 --> Q1["Public Consultation Mandate"]
    Q1 --> R1["Environmental Clearance EC"]
    R1 --> S1["NGT Act 2010 Enforcement"]
    K3["Forest Conservation Act 1980"] --> S2["Forest Clearance Parallel to EC"]
    K4["Wildlife Protection Act 1972"] --> S3["Wildlife Clearance Parallel to EC"]
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This concept map can be drawn directly in answer sheets for questions asking *"Explain the legislative framework of EIA in India"* (a 7-mark question).

---

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Validity Periods of Environmental Clearance

| Project Category | EC Validity | CT Validity | Re-Application Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Category A | **7 years** | Up to **30 years** | New application after expiry |
| Category B1 | **7 years** | Up to **30 years** | New application after expiry |
| Category B2 | **5 years** | Up to **25 years** | New application after expiry |
| Mining Projects | **2 years** (validity of ToR) | Project life | Renewal needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **7-year validity** rule is one of the most commonly asked KTU question — memorize it precisely.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks

**Define the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 and explain its significance as an umbrella legislation for EIA in India.**

**Model Answer:**

The Environment (Protection) Act, **1986** (EPA) is an **umbrella legislation** enacted by the Parliament of India under **Article 253** of the Constitution. It was enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** and the **Stockholm Conference (1972)** to provide for the protection and improvement of the environment and to handle matters connected therewith.

**Significance for EIA:**

1. **Legal Basis:** EPA provides the *parent statutory authority* under which the **EIA Notification 2006** was issued.
2. **Regulatory Power:** Section 5 empowers the Central Government to prescribe **standards for emissions and effluents**, regulate industrial locations, and even close non-compliant industries.
3. **Penalty Mechanism:** Section 15 prescribes imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, plus a daily fine of up to **₹5,000** for continuing violations.
4. **Coordination:** It coordinates all sectoral acts (Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981) and notifications under a single legal umbrella.

**Concluding Sentence:** Without EPA 1986, the Indian EIA framework would lack enforceable statutory force.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks

**Differentiate between the Water Act, 1974 and the Air Act, 1981.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Water Act, 1974 | Air Act, 1981 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Medium regulated | Water bodies & streams | Atmospheric air |
| Statutory bodies | Created **CPCB & SPCBs** | Empowered existing **SPCBs** |
| Key mechanism | Consent (CTE & CTO) | Consent (CTE & CTO) |
| Standards enforced | Effluent discharge standards | Ambient air & emission standards |
| Significant amendment | **1988** | **1987** |

**Conclusion:** Both acts work in conjunction with EPA 1986 and are essential pre-conditions for the EIA clearance process.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the **EIA Notification, 2006**. Discuss the **project categorization** under it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **powers and functions of the National Green Tribunal (NGT)** under the NGT Act, 2010. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a):

The **EIA Notification, 2006** (S.O. 1533(E), dated 14th September 2006) issued by MoEF under Section 3(1) and Section 5 of **EPA, 1986**, is the **operational backbone** of EIA in India.

**Salient Features:**

1. **Mandatory Prior EC** — Projects listed in Schedule I cannot commence without prior Environmental Clearance.
2. **Project Categorization** — Projects are classified into:
   - **Category A:** Appraised at the **Central level** by **EAC** under MoEFCC.
   - **Category B:** Appraised at the **State level** by **SEAC** and approved by **SEIAA**, further divided into **B1** (EIA required) and **B2** (Form 1 only).
3. **Screening, Scoping, Public Consultation, Appraisal** — Four-stage EIA process.
4. **Validity** — EC valid for **7 years** for commencement of operations.
5. **Compliance Monitoring** — Six-monthly compliance reports mandatory.
6. **Public Consultation** — Mandatory for Category A and B1; exempted for B2, modernization, and B2 defense projects.

**Project Categorization (Examples):**

| Category | Examples |
| :--- | :--- |
| Category A | Mining (>50 ha), thermal power (>500 MW), highways (≥100 km) |
| Category B1 | Building construction ≥20,000 sq.m, townships |
| Category B2 | Small-scale industries with low pollution index |

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b):

The **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** was established on **18th October 2010** under the **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010** for the effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to **environmental protection, conservation of forests and natural resources**, and enforcement of any legal right relating to the environment.

**Powers and Functions:**

1. **Original Jurisdiction:** NGT has the power to hear **all civil cases** where a substantial question relating to the environment is involved.
2. **Appellate Jurisdiction:** NGT hears appeals against orders of statutory authorities (SPCB, MoEF, etc.).
3. **No Civil Court Bar:** Section 19 bars civil courts from entertaining environmental disputes.
4. **Special Bench Composition:** Each bench consists of a **Judicial Member** and an **Expert Member**.
5. **Relief Available:**
   - **Compensation** for victims of pollution (Section 15).
   - **Restitution** of damaged environment.
   - **Injunctions** to stop ongoing damage.
6. **Penalty Powers:** Can impose fines and direct compliance.
7. **Time-Bound Adjudication:** Cases must be decided within **6 months**.
8. **Five Benches:** Principal Bench at **New Delhi**, regional benches at Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai.

**Landmark NGT Verdict:** The *Sterlite Industries* (Tuticorin) order (2019) is a prime example of NGT exercising its powers to enforce environmental compliance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Wildlife Protection Act, 1972** and **Forest Conservation Act, 1980**. Explain their relevance in EIA studies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **constitutional provisions** for environmental protection in India. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a):

**Wildlife Protection Act, 1972:**

- Enacted to provide for the **protection of wild animals, birds, and plants** and for matters connected therewith.
- Created the **Protected Area (PA) network** — National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves, Community Reserves.
- Schedules I to VI classify animals based on protection levels (Schedule I = highest protection).
- Established the **Central Zoo Authority (CZA)** in 1992 for oversight of zoological parks.

**Relevance in EIA:**

- EIA studies must assess impacts on wildlife if the project is within **10 km** of a Protected Area.
- Wildlife Institute of India (WII) is often consulted for habitat assessments.

**Forest Conservation Act, 1980:**

- Enacted to check deforestation and diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes.
- Requires **prior approval** of the Central Government for diverting forest land.
- The Forest Advisory Committee (FAC) advises on such diversions.

**Relevance in EIA:**

- Projects involving forest diversion require **dual clearance** — Environmental Clearance (under EPA 1986) AND **Forest Clearance (under FC Act 1980)**.
- Compensatory afforestation is mandatory.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b):

**Constitutional Provisions for Environmental Protection:**

The Indian Constitution, through the **42nd Amendment Act, 1976**, incorporated environmental protection into its basic structure:

**Article 48A (Part IV — Directive Principles of State Policy):**

> *"The State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country."*

- Enjoins the **State** to take positive action for environmental conservation.
- Not directly enforceable in court but is **fundamental in governance**.

**Article 51A(g) (Part IVA — Fundamental Duties):**

> *"It shall be the duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to have compassion for living creatures."*

- Imposes a **fundamental duty on every citizen**.
- Becomes enforceable through enabling legislation (e.g., EPA 1986).

**Judicial Innovations:**

- **Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996):** Introduced the **Polluter Pays Principle** and **Precautionary Principle** as part of Indian environmental law.
- **M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Ganga Pollution Case):** Established that industries must compensate for environmental damage.

**Conclusion:** The Constitutional provisions provide the **philosophical foundation** upon which all EIA legislations rest.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Umbrella vs. Sectoral Acts:** EPA 1986 is the umbrella; Water/Air Acts are sectoral. Do not interchange.
> 2. **Year Mistakes:** The Air Act is **1981** (not 1982). EIA Notification is **2006** (not 1994 — that was the older one). CRZ Notification is **2019** (latest revision).
> 3. **Missing the Constitutional Basis:** Always mention **Article 48A and Article 51A(g)** when discussing environmental law.
> 4. **Forgetting NGT Validity:** NGT was established in **2010**, NOT 1995 (that was the National Environment Tribunal).
> 5. **Public Consultation Confusion:** Public Hearing is **mandatory for A and B1** but **exempted for B2 and modernization projects**.
> 6. **Penalty Calculation:** Use **₹1 lakh + ₹5,000/day** formula precisely. Many students mix up the figures.
> 7. **Forest vs Wildlife Clearance:** These are **parallel** to EC, not substitutes. A project may need **all three clearances**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EIA Legislations:**

- ☐ **EPA 1986** is the **umbrella legislation** under Article 253.
- ☐ **Water Act 1974** created **CPCB & SPCBs**; consent mechanism (CTE & CTO).
- ☐ **Air Act 1981** regulates **atmospheric pollution**; amended 1987.
- ☐ **Wildlife Protection Act 1972** — Schedule I to VI; **Protected Areas**.
- ☐ **Forest Conservation Act 1980** — **Forest Clearance (FC)** mandatory for forest diversion.
- ☐ **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** — mandatory insurance for hazardous industries.
- ☐ **Biological Diversity Act 2002** — NBA, SBBs; implements **CBD**.
- ☐ **EIA Notification 2006** — mandatory EIA framework; **Category A and B (B1, B2)**.
- ☐ **NGT Act 2010** — Tribunal for environmental disputes; 5 benches.
- ☐ **CRZ Notification 2019** — Coastal Regulation Zone rules.
- ☐ **Articles 48A & 51A(g)** — Constitutional foundation.
- ☐ **Polluter Pays & Precautionary Principles** — judicial innovations (1996).
- ☐ **Penalty under EPA:** ₹1 lakh + ₹5,000/day (Section 15).
- ☐ **EC Validity:** **7 years** for Category A and B1.
- ☐ **6-monthly compliance reports** mandatory under EIA Notification 2006.
- ☐ **Public Consultation** mandatory for Category A and B1; exempted for B2.
- ☐ **10 km buffer rule** for projects near Ecologically Sensitive Areas (ESAs).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Liner Final Summary:**
> *"Environmental Impact Assessment in India is legally anchored by the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, supported by sectoral laws (Water 1974, Air 1981, Wildlife 1972, Forest 1980), operationalized by the EIA Notification 2006, and adjudicated by the National Green Tribunal under the NGT Act, 2010 — all deriving philosophical force from Articles 48A and 51A(g) of the Indian Constitution."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws and Acts Relevant to Environmental Protection in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Laws and Acts Relevant to Environmental Protection in India ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Environmental Legislation** refers to the codified body of statutory laws, rules, notifications, and amendments enacted by the Parliament and State Legislatures of India, along with the executive orders issued by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), designed to regulate, conserve, protect, and improve the natural and human environment. Under the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic establishes the legal scaffolding without which the entire process of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** would lack enforceability. In the Indian constitutional framework, environmental protection is implicitly derived from **Article 48A** (Directive Principles of State Policy) and **Article 51A(g)** (Fundamental Duties), which collectively obligate the State to protect the environment and compel every citizen to safeguard wildlife, compassion for living creatures, and the natural ecosystem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to critically appraise the jurisdictional scope, penal provisions, regulatory authorities (like CPCB, SPCB, NGT, MoEFCC), and the practical implementation of these acts within an EIA project lifecycle. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of India's environmental laws as a **multi-layered shield** guarding a medieval castle (the ecosystem). Each act is a different section of the wall: - The **Water Act, 1974** is the moat that controls liquid waste. - The **Air Act, 1981** is the air filtration system preventing toxic gases from poisoning the inhabitants. - The **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** is the **omnipotent umbrella** — the commander-in-chief that gives the central government sweeping power to issue new rules whenever a new environmental threat emerges. - The **Wild Life Protection Act, 1972** is the zoo keeper ensuring biodiversity survives. - The **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010** is the **fast-track courtroom** specifically built to resolve environmental disputes quickly, bypassing the decades-long wait in normal Indian courts. Without these laws, EIA would simply be a voluntary report that developers could ignore with zero consequences. > [!NOTE] > **Core Statutory Bodies Created by these Acts:** > - **CPCB** – Central Pollution Control Board > - **SPCBs** – State Pollution Control Boards > - **NGT** – National Green Tribunal > - **CZA** – Central Zoo Authority > - **NBWL** – National Board for Wildlife > - **NBA** – National Biodiversity Authority ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Categorical Framework of Environmental Legislation Indian environmental laws can be logically grouped into **four functional pillars**: **Pillar I: Pollution Control Legislation** Designed to regulate the discharge of contaminants into specific environmental media. - The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 - The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977 - The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 - The Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016 (originally 1989) **Pillar II: Umbrella / Framework Legislation** Provides the overarching legal authority to address any environmental issue. - The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (the "Mother Act" post-Bhopal Disaster) **Pillar III: Conservation of Natural Resources & Ecology** Protects specific natural assets. - The Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972 - The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 - The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 - The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 - Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 2019 **Pillar IV: Liability, Compensation & Adjudication** Establishes accountability and dispute resolution. - The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 - The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 - The Public Liability Insurance Rules, 1991 ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Key Acts at a Glance | Act / Rule | Year | Key Authority Created | Core Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Wildlife (Protection) Act | 1972 | NBWL, CZA | Protect wild animals, birds, plants; schedules I–VI | | Water (P&CP) Act | 1974 | CPCB, SPCBs | Prevent & control water pollution; consent to establish/operate | | Water Cess Act | 1977 | — | Levy cess on water consumed by industries to fund CPCB/SPCBs | | Forest (Conservation) Act | 1980 | — | Restrict state use of forest land for non-forest purposes | | Air (P&CP) Act | 1981 | CPCB, SPCBs | Prevent, control, abate air pollution; ambient air quality standards | | Environment (Protection) Act | 1986 | MoEFCC | Umbrella act post-Bhopal; regulate EIA, coastal zones, hazardous waste | | Hazardous Wastes Rules | 1989 | SPCB | Management, handling, storage, transport of hazardous waste | | Public Liability Insurance Act | 1991 | — | Mandatory insurance for industries handling hazardous substances | | National Environment Tribunal Act | 1995 | NET | Adjudicate compensation claims *(repealed by NGT Act, 2010)* | | Biological Diversity Act | 2002 | NBA, SBB | Conservation of biodiversity, sustainable use, equitable benefit sharing | | Forest Rights Act | 2006 | Gram Sabha | Recognize rights of tribal/forest dwellers over forest land | | National Green Tribunal Act | 2010 | NGT | Fast-track adjudication of environmental disputes | > [!NOTE] > **Vital Constitutional Provisions for EIA Context:** > - **Article 14** – Right to Equality (life in polluted environment violates this) > - **Article 19(1)(a)** – Right to Information (includes right to know about EIA) > - **Article 21** – Right to Life and Personal Liberty (expanded to include right to clean environment via *Subhash Kumar v. State of Bihar*, 1991) > - **Article 32 / 226** – Writ jurisdiction for environmental PILs ### 2.3 Engineering Utility in EIA Projects In any EIA study, the consultant **must** map every project activity against the applicable legal provisions. For instance: - A thermal power plant requires **Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO)** under both the Water Act, 1974 and Air Act, 1981. - A project within **10 km of a national park/sanctuary** triggers the Wild Life (Protection) Act clearance through the Standing Committee of NBWL. - A project involving **forest land diversion** requires prior approval under the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980. - A project with capital investment ≥ ₹50 crores (sector-specific thresholds) triggers the **EIA Notification, 2006** under the EP Act, 1986. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Major Acts Since this is a legislative topic, the "derivations" are replaced with **structured legal analyses** of the most frequently examined statutes. ### 3.1 The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 **Step 1: Origin and Trigger** Enacted to give effect to the recommendations of the National Water Pollution Control Council. It was the **first comprehensive Indian legislation** on pollution control, predating the Air Act. **Step 2: Institutional Framework** - Section 3: Constitution of the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**. - Section 4: Constitution of **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** in every state/UT. - Section 17: Functions of CPCB include coordinating state boards, collecting water quality data, and laying down effluent standards. **Step 3: Regulatory Mechanism** - **Sections 25 & 26**: No person shall establish/operate any industry likely to discharge sewage/trade effluent into a stream without prior **Consent** from the SPCB. - **Two-stage consent**: - **Consent to Establish (CTE)** — before construction. - **Consent to Operate (CTO)** — before commissioning and renewable. **Step 4: Penalty Provisions** - **Section 24**: Penalty for contravention — imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to ₹10,000, or both. Repeat offense: 6 months and ₹5,000 per day. ### 3.2 The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977 **Step 1: Rationale** The Water Act, 1974 created SPCBs but provided no dedicated funding. This Act was passed to **levy a financial cess** on industries consuming water, thereby creating a self-sustaining revenue model. **Step 2: Mechanism** - **Section 3**: Levy and collection of cess on water consumed by specified industries. - **Section 6**: The revenue is shared between CPCB and SPCBs in a prescribed ratio. - **Section 8**: A rebate is provided to industries that install effluent treatment plants — this is a strong **economic incentive** for pollution control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The 1977 Cess Act is a classic KTU question because it links **environmental economics** to **legal compliance**. ### 3.3 The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 **Step 1: Constitutional Basis** Article 253 (Union List Entry 6 of VII Schedule) — powers to legislate on inter-state pollution matters. **Step 2: Extension of Water Act Framework** - Sections 4 and 5 empower Central and State Governments to designate air pollution control areas. - **Section 17** makes CPCB and SPCBs the apex bodies for air quality monitoring and standard-setting. - **Section 21**: Establishments cannot operate without prior consent of SPCB. **Step 3: National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** CPCB notified NAAQS in 1982 (revised 1994 and 2009) for 12 pollutants including PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, and Benzene. **Step 4: Penalties** - Section 26: First offense — fine up to ₹10,000 or imprisonment up to 3 months. - Subsequent offense — fine up to ₹5,000/day and imprisonment up to 6 months. ### 3.4 The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (The "Umbrella Act") **Step 1: Historical Catalyst** Triggered by the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)** — the world's worst industrial disaster, which exposed the inadequacy of sector-specific laws. Enacted under Article 253 following the **Stockholm Conference (1972)** and the **Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act, 1985**. **Step 2: Sweeping Powers (Section 3)** The Central Government is empowered to take **all necessary measures** to: - Coordinate actions of state governments under various environmental laws. - Lay down environmental quality standards for air, water, soil, noise. - Prescribe procedures and safeguards for handling hazardous substances. - Inspect industrial units, install pollution monitoring equipment, and issue closure orders. **Step 3: Major Notifications under EP Act, 1986** This is the most critical aspect for KTU exams: - **EIA Notification, 2006** (and 2009 amendments): Mandates prior environmental clearance for 30+ industrial sectors. - **Hazardous Wastes (Management & Handling) Rules, 1989** (replaced by 2008 Rules, then 2016 Rules). - **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 1991** (revised 2011, 2019). - **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** (revised 2016). - **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** (amended 2017). - **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2011, 2016, 2022**. **Step 4: Penalty Provisions** - **Section 15**: Imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both; additional fine up to ₹5,000/day for continuing violations. - **Section 16**: Offenses are **cognizable, bailable, and non-compoundable**. ### 3.5 The National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 **Step 1: Genesis** - Established in accordance with Article 21 (Right to Clean Environment) and Article 48A. - Came into effect on **1st October 2010** (formally constituted 18 May 2011). - Arose from the recommendations of the **Law Commission of India (186th Report, 2003)**. **Step 2: Structure** - **Principal Bench** at New Delhi. - **Regional Benches** at Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai. - Comprises a **Judicial Member**, an **Expert Member**, and is chaired by the **Chairperson** (a retired Supreme Court or High Court Chief Justice). **Step 3: Jurisdiction (Section 14–17)** The NGT has jurisdiction over **all civil cases** where a substantial question relating to the environment is involved, including: - Water and air pollution. - Forest conservation and wildlife protection. - Enforcement of legal rights relating to the environment. - Damages to persons, property, or the environment from industrial activity. **Step 4: Adjudication Period (Section 19)** Cases must be disposed of within **6 months** of filing — a stark contrast to decades-long delays in conventional Indian courts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Precedential Foundation:** In *M.C. Mehta v. Union of India* (Oleum Gas Leak case, 1987), the Supreme Court established the principle of **Absolute Liability** for hazardous industries — this principle is now operationally enforced through the NGT. ### 3.6 The Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972 (Amended 2002, 2006) **Step 1: Pre-Legislative Context** - India became a signatory to the **Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES)** in 1976. - The Act provides for the **protection of wild animals, birds, and plants** and matters connected therewith. **Step 2: Schedules of Protected Species** - **Schedule I**: Highest protection (e.g., Tiger, Lion, Great Indian Bustard) — highest penalties. - **Schedule II**: High protection. - **Schedule III & IV**: Protected species. - **Schedule V**: **Vermins** (can be hunted). - **Schedule VI**: Protected plants. **Step 3: Protected Areas (Chapter IV)** - **Sanctuaries** (Section 26): Activities regulated, not totally prohibited. - **National Parks** (Section 35): Greater degree of protection, no human activity allowed. - **Conservation Reserves** and **Community Reserves** (added in 2002 amendment). **Step 4: Authorities Established** - **National Board for Wildlife (NBWL)** — chaired by the PM. - **Wildlife Advisory Boards** at the state level. - **Central Zoo Authority (CZA)** for regulation of zoos. **Step 5: Penalties** Hunting Schedule I animals: imprisonment **3–7 years** and fine not less than ₹25,000. ### 3.7 The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 (Amended 1996, 2003, 2017) **Step 1: Pre-Legislative Context** Enacted in response to the **Godavarman vs. Union of India (1996)** precedent and historic deforestation concerns. **Step 2: Core Mandate** **No state government can de-reserve a forest, use forest land for non-forest purposes, or lease forest land to private parties without prior approval of the Central Government.** **Step 3: Compliance Pathway** 1. State Government submits proposal to the MoEFCC. 2. Compensatory Afforestation is required (1:1 ratio minimum). 3. Net Present Value (NPV) is collected from user agency. 4. Project proponent creates a **Forest Conservation Plan**. ### 3.8 EIA Notification, 2006 — The Operational Backbone This is the **most frequently tested notification** in KTU exams. **Step 1: Legal Basis** Issued under **Section 3(1)(v) and 3(2)(ii) of the EP Act, 1986**, and SO 1533(E) dated 14 September 2006. **Step 2: Project Classification** - **Category A**: Projects at the border of or within specific zones (Eco-sensitive Zones, Critically Polluted Areas, within 10 km of critically polluted areas) — cleared by the **MoEFCC at the central level**. - **Category B**: Projects not in sensitive zones — cleared by the **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**. **Step 3: Sub-Categorization of Category B** - **B1**: Requires full EIA report. - **B2**: Requires only a simplified form (no public consultation). **Step 4: EIA Process (Six Steps)** 1. Screening 2. Scoping 3. Baseline Data Collection 4. Impact Prediction and Assessment 5. Mitigation Measures and EIA Report Preparation 6. Public Consultation and Appraisal ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Indian Environmental Law ```mermaid graph TD constitution["Constitution of India Articles 14 21 48A 51A g 253"]:::core goa["EP Act 1986 Umbrella Act"]:::core wpca["Water Act 1974"]:::pillar1 wca["Water Cess Act 1977"]:::pillar1 apca["Air Act 1981"]:::pillar1 hwr["Hazardous Waste Rules 2016"]:::pillar1 wlp["Wildlife Protection Act 1972"]:::pillar2 fca["Forest Conservation Act 1980"]:::pillar2 bda["Biological Diversity Act 2002"]:::pillar2 cra["CRZ Notification 2019"]:::pillar2 fra["Forest Rights Act 2006"]:::pillar2 pli["Public Liability Insurance Act 1991"]:::pillar3 ngta["NGT Act 2010"]:::pillar3 eia["EIA Notification 2006"]:::core eiaaa["EIA Amendment 2020 Draft"]:::core constitution --> goa goa --> wpca goa --> apca goa --> hwr goa --> eia goa --> eiaaa goa --> cra wpca --> wca goa --> wlp goa --> fca goa --> bda goa --> fra goa --> pli goa --> ngta classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef pillar1 fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef pillar2 fill:#5a3d2d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef pillar3 fill:#5a2d3d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 EIA Project Clearance Workflow under EPA 1986 ```mermaid graph LR startA["Project Proposal Submitted"]:::node1 screen{"Screening
Category A or B"}:::node2 catA["Category A
Central Appraisal"]:::node3 catB["Category B
State Appraisal via SEIAA"]:::node4 scope["Scoping and
Terms of Reference"]:::node5 base["Baseline Data

and Prediction"]:::node7 pub["Public Consultation
