Reasons"]:::node13 startA --> screen screen -->|Category A| catA screen -->|Category B| catB catA --> scope catB --> scope scope --> base base --> impact impact --> report report --> pub pub --> eac eac -->|Approved| grant eac -->|Rejected| reject grant --> monitor classDef node1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef node2 fill:#5a4a1f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef node3 fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node4 fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node5 fill:#3d2d5a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node6 fill:#3d2d5a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node7 fill:#3d2d5a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node8 fill:#3d2d5a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node9 fill:#3d2d5a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node10 fill:#5a1f3d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node11 fill:#1f5a3d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef node12 fill:#1f5a3d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff classDef node13 fill:#5a1f1f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 NGT Jurisdiction and Function Map ```mermaid graph TD ngthead["NGT Chairperson Retired CJI or HC CJ"]:::head judmem["Judicial Member"]:::body expmem["Expert Member"]:::body pbench["Principal Bench New Delhi"]:::body rbench1["Regional Bench Bhopal"]:::body rbench2["Regional Bench Pune"]:::body rbench3["Regional Bench Kolkata"]:::body rbench4["Regional Bench Chennai"]:::body civil1["Water Pollution Cases"]:::body civil2["Air Pollution Cases"]:::body civil3["Forest and Wildlife Cases"]:::body civil4["Hazardous Substance Damage"]:::body civil5["Contravention of EIA Conditions"]:::body ngthead --> judmem ngthead --> expmem ngthead --> pbench ngthead --> rbench1 ngthead --> rbench2 ngthead --> rbench3 ngthead --> rbench4 pbench --> civil1 pbench --> civil2 rbench1 --> civil3 rbench2 --> civil4 rbench3 --> civil5 classDef head fill:#5a1f3d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef body fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Penalty Matrix ```mermaid graph LR epact["EP Act 1986
5 yrs and Rs 1 lakh"]:::penalty water["Water Act 1974
3 months and Rs 10000"]:::penalty air["Air Act 1981
3 months and Rs 10000"]:::penalty wild["Wildlife Act 1972
Schedule I 3 to 7 yrs and Rs 25000"]:::penalty cess["Water Cess Act 1977
Penalty for non payment"]:::penalty plia["PLI Act 1991
Compensation up to Rs 25 crores"]:::penalty biot["Biodiversity Act 2002
3 yrs and Rs 5 lakhs"]:::penalty classDef penalty fill:#5a2d1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by the "Umbrella Act" in Indian environmental legislation? Name the Act and justify why it earned this title.* **Model Answer:** The "Umbrella Act" refers to the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. It is called so because, unlike previous sector-specific laws, it empowers the Central Government to take **all measures deemed necessary** to protect and improve the environment, and to lay down standards, procedures, and safeguards for virtually **all environmental domains** — air, water, soil, noise, hazardous waste, and industrial safety. It was enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** and provides the legal foundation for major notifications like the **EIA Notification, 2006** and **CRZ Notification, 2019**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the composition of the National Green Tribunal (NGT) and the time limit for disposing of cases.* **Model Answer:** The NGT comprises a **Chairperson** (a retired Judge of the Supreme Court or Chief Justice of a High Court), **Judicial Members**, and **Expert Members** having adequate knowledge of environmental law, science, or engineering. The Tribunal must dispose of cases within a period of **six months** from the date of filing, ensuring swift justice in environmental disputes. The Principal Bench is in New Delhi, with regional benches at Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the salient features, institutional setup, and penal provisions of the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 was enacted under **Article 253 of the Constitution** to implement the decisions of the **United Nations Conference on Human Environment (Stockholm, 1972)**. **1. Salient Features:** - It extends the framework of the Water Act, 1974 to the atmosphere. - The Central Government may declare any area as an **"Air Pollution Control Area"** (Sections 4 and 5). - The Act aims to **prevent, control, and abate** air pollution. **2. Institutional Setup:** - **CPCB** (constituted earlier under the Water Act, 1974) is the apex body at the national level. - **SPCBs** are empowered to enforce the Act at the state level. - Functions: advise Central/State Governments, coordinate activities, collect air quality data, and lay down standards for emissions. **3. Consent Mechanism (Section 21):** No person shall establish or operate any industrial plant without prior **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** from the relevant SPCB. **4. NAAQS:** The CPCB notified **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** in 1982, revised in 1994 and 2009, covering 12 pollutants including PM2.5, PM10, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, and Benzene. **5. Penal Provisions (Section 26):** - First offense: imprisonment up to **3 months** or fine up to **₹10,000**, or both. - Continued contravention: additional fine of **₹5,000 per day**. - Repeat offense: imprisonment up to **6 months**. --- **(b)** Discuss the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977** and highlight its significance in funding the regulatory bodies. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Cess Act, 1977 was enacted as a **companion financial legislation** to the Water Act, 1974. **1. Rationale:** The Water Act, 1974 created CPCB and SPCBs but provided no dedicated funding source. The 1977 Act fills this gap by levying a **cess on water consumed** by industries specified in the Schedule. **2. Key Provisions:** - **Section 3**: Levy and collection of cess. - **Section 4**: Filing of returns by industrial units. - **Section 6**: Distribution of revenue between CPCB and SPCBs. - **Section 8**: A **rebate is provided** to industries that install effluent treatment plants — this incentivizes pollution control by reducing the effective cess. **3. Significance:** - Provides financial autonomy to CPCB and SPCBs. - Acts as an **economic instrument** to encourage in-house pollution abatement. - The 2017 amendment rationalized the rate structure. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** Describe the genesis, objectives, and key provisions of the **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010**, and explain how it differs from a conventional civil court. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **1. Genesis:** - Article 21 of the Constitution guarantees the right to life, interpreted by the Supreme Court to include the right to a clean environment. - The **Law Commission of India (186th Report, 2003)** recommended a dedicated environmental tribunal. - NGT was established on **1 October 2010** under the NGT Act, 2010. **2. Objectives:** - Effective and expeditious disposal of environmental cases. - Provide relief and compensation to victims of environmental damage. - Strengthen environmental jurisprudence. **3. Key Provisions:** - **Section 14–17**: Jurisdiction over all civil cases with substantial environmental questions, including water, air, forest, and wildlife matters. - **Section 19**: Disposal of cases within **6 months**. - **Section 20**: Orders and decisions are **binding**, and non-compliance is punishable as contempt. **4. Differences from Conventional Civil Court:** | Feature | Civil Court | NGT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Duration | Years to decades | 6 months maximum | | Composition | Only judicial members | Judicial + Expert members | | Technical expertise | Limited | Multi-disciplinary | | Compensation mechanism | Slow, bureaucratic | Fast, scientific assessment | | Jurisdiction | General | Exclusive on environmental issues | --- **(b)** Outline the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** in detail, including its constitutional basis, key powers conferred on the Central Government, and the major notifications issued under it. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **1. Constitutional Basis:** Enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution to give effect to the UN Stockholm Declaration (1972) and to address the regulatory gap exposed by the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)**. **2. Key Powers (Section 3) — The Central Government may:** - Coordinate actions of State Governments and various regulatory boards. - Lay down **environmental quality standards** for air, water, soil, and noise. - Prescribe procedures and safeguards for handling hazardous substances. - Conduct inspections, install monitoring devices, and issue closure orders to non-compliant units. **3. Major Notifications under EP Act, 1986:** - **EIA Notification, 2006** – mandates prior environmental clearance. - **Hazardous Wastes (Management and Handling) Rules, 1989** (replaced by 2016 Rules). - **Municipal Solid Waste Rules, 2000** (revised 2016). - **Coastal Regulation Zone Notification, 1991** (revised 2011, 2019). - **Noise Pollution Rules, 2000** (amended 2017). - **E-Waste Management Rules, 2011/2016/2022**. **4. Penalties (Section 15):** - Imprisonment up to **5 years** or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, or both. - Additional fine of **₹5,000 per day** for continuing violations. - Offenses are **cognizable**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > Students frequently lose 2–3 marks on the following points: > 1. **Confusing the Water Act, 1974 and Water Cess Act, 1977.** Remember: 1974 = regulatory (consent), 1977 = financial (cess). > 2. **Forgetting the Bhopal connection to the EP Act, 1986** — without this historical context, the Act's "umbrella" nature makes no sense. > 3. **Mixing up NGT benches** — the Principal Bench is in **New Delhi**, regional benches in Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai. > 4. **Confusing Wildlife Schedules** — Schedule V is the **Vermin list** (animals that can be hunted). > 5. **Forgetting to mention the 6-month disposal rule** of the NGT — this is a KTU favorite. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Article 21** is the constitutional bedrock — *Subhash Kumar vs. State of Bihar (1991)* established the right to a clean environment. - The **EP Act, 1986** is the **most powerful** environmental law because it covers all media and empowers the Central Government with quasi-legislative authority. - The **Water Act, 1974** was the **first** pollution control law; the **Air Act, 1981** borrowed its institutional framework. - The **EIA Notification, 2006** is the operational backbone — projects in **Category A** need central clearance; **Category B** is split into B1 (full EIA) and B2 (simplified). - The **NGT** must dispose of cases in **6 months** and has its Principal Bench in New Delhi with regional benches in **Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, and Chennai**. - The **Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972** has **6 schedules**; Schedule I species (e.g., Tiger) attract the highest penalties (3–7 years imprisonment). - The **Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980** requires **prior central approval** for any non-forest use of forest land. - The **Biological Diversity Act, 2002** created the **National Biodiversity Authority (NBA)** to govern access to Indian genetic resources and ensure equitable benefit sharing with local communities. - The **Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991** mandates industries handling hazardous substances to maintain mandatory insurance for immediate relief to accident victims. - The **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** replaced the 1989 Rules and govern the import, export, and transit of hazardous waste under the **Basel Convention** obligations. - The **CRZ Notification, 2019** classifies coastal zones into four categories (CRZ-I to CRZ-IVA) with progressively relaxed development norms. - The **Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006** empowers **Gram Sabhas** to grant individual and community forest rights, sometimes conflicting with FC Act, 1980 clearances — a frequent KTU exam debate. - **Penalties** — EP Act 1986 (5 years / ₹1 lakh) is the strictest; Water/Air Acts (3 months / ₹10,000) are relatively lenient. - **Liability Principle** — *M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak, 1987)* established **Absolute Liability** (no escape via "no fault"). - **Polluter Pays Principle** — endorsed by the Supreme Court in *Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996)*, requires the polluter to bear the cost of remediation.

topics.title: The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (EPA, 1986)** is an **Umbrella Legislation** enacted by the Parliament of India on **23rd May 1986** (came into force on **19th November 1986**) under Article 253 of the Constitution, in the wake of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**. It is officially cited as **Act No. 29 of 1986** and serves as the primary statutory framework empowering the **Central Government** to coordinate, regulate, and protect the environment and to establish authorities for the prevention, control, and abatement of environmental pollution.

The Act is often called the *"Framework Law"* or *"Umbrella Act"* of Indian environmental jurisprudence because it consolidates the powers of the Central Government to deal with **all types of environmental pollution** (air, water, land, noise) and to lay down **standards, procedures, and safeguards**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Supreme Court of Indian Environment"**
>
> Imagine Indian environmental laws as a **specialized hospital system**:
> - The **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** = a heart specialist
> - The **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** = a lung specialist
> - The **EPA, 1986** = the **Central Hospital Authority** that has **overriding powers over every specialist department**. When a new disease (pollution type) appears that no existing specialist can handle (e.g., e-waste, hazardous chemicals, biomedical waste, noise pollution, plastic pollution), the Central Authority steps in, issues new protocols, and mandates treatment for all.

After the Bhopal disaster exposed the severe inadequacy of sector-specific laws (which covered only water and air at that time), Parliament realized that India needed **one overarching law** that could address any kind of environmental harm — past, present, and future. EPA, 1986 was that response.

## 1.3 Constitutional & Historical Backdrop

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Constitutional Provision** | Article 253 (Parliament's power to legislate on matters in State List to implement international agreements) |
| **Triggering Event** | Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Union Carbide, 2-3 Dec 1984) |
| **Stockholm Conference** | India hosted & signed the 1972 UN Conference on Human Environment; EPA implements these obligations |
| **Pre-existing Laws** | Water Act (1974), Air Act (1981) — only covered water/air pollution |
| **Scope of EPA 1986** | Expanded to include **all environmental pollutants** — air, water, land, noise, hazardous substances |
| **Year of Enactment** | **1986** |
| **Applicable Territory** | Whole of India (extends to J&K post-1972) |

## 1.4 Key Definitions (Section 2 of the Act)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2** of the Act provides the foundational definitions that drive the entire statute:

- **"Environment"** — includes **water, air, land** and the inter-relationship which exists among and between water, air and land, and human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms and property.
- **"Environmental Pollutant"** — means any solid, liquid or gaseous substance present in such concentration as may be, or tend to be, injurious to environment.
- **"Environmental Pollution"** — means the presence of any environmental pollutant in the environment.
- **"Hazardous Substance"** — means any substance or preparation which, by reason of its chemical or physico-chemical properties or handling, is liable to cause harm to human beings, other living creatures, plants, micro-organisms, property or the environment.
- **"Occupier"** — in relation to any factory or premises, means the person who has the ultimate control over the affairs of the factory or premises.
- **"Prescribed"** — means prescribed by rules made under this Act.

## 1.5 Salient Features (One-Line Snapshot)

> [!NOTE]
> - It is an **enabling Act** — gives power to the Central Government to issue rules/orders.
> - It is **comprehensive** in scope, covering every form of pollution.
> - It is the **legal backbone** for issuing EIA Notifications (1994, 2006, 2020).
> - It applies to the **whole of India**.
> - It does **NOT** directly specify penalties — penalties are governed by **Section 24** (referencing IPC sections).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why Was EPA 1986 Needed? — The "Why" Behind the Act

The Act was the legislative fallout of a **triple crisis**:

1. **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Dec 1984)** — Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) leak killed 3,000+ people, injured 500,000+. Exposed absence of strict laws on hazardous industries.
2. **Ozone Depletion Crisis (1985)** — Vienna Convention signed; needed domestic law.
3. **Stockholm Declaration (1972) commitment** — India needed a national mechanism to implement international environmental obligations.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Stockholm Conference** gave rise to the **National Council for Environmental Policy and Planning (1982)** and then a full-fledged **Ministry of Environment, Forests and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**. The EPA provided the legal teeth for this ministry to act.

## 2.2 Structural Pillars of the Act

The Act is structured into **26 Sections + 3 Schedules**. The operational logic can be broken into 5 pillars:

### Pillar 1: Power to Coordinate (Sections 3–4)
- **Section 3**: Central Government can take all such measures as it deems necessary to protect and improve the environment and to prevent, control and abate environmental pollution.
- **Section 3(2)(a–h)**: Power to coordinate activities of State Governments, officers, and authorities under the Act.

### Pillar 2: Power to Plan & Execute (Section 3)
- The Central Government can plan and execute a **nation-wide programme** for the prevention, control and abatement of environmental pollution.

### Pillar 3: Power to Set Standards (Sections 6, 7)
- **Section 6**: Power to prescribe **standards for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants** from any source — industries, automobiles, etc.
- **Section 7**: Power to prescribe **standards for the quality of environment** in its various aspects (ambient air quality, water quality, noise).

### Pillar 4: Power to Restrict / Regulate (Sections 7A, 8, 9, 10, 11)
- **Section 7A**: Power to issue directions for closure, prohibition, or regulation of any industry, operation, or process on environmental grounds.
- **Section 8**: Handlers of hazardous substances must comply with procedural safeguards.
- **Section 9**: Power to lay down **rules** for handling hazardous substances.
- **Section 10**: Power to appoint **Inspectorate** for environmental monitoring.
- **Section 11**: Power to give **directions** to the in-charge of any industry, including directions for the **closure of the industry**.

### Pillar 5: Penalties (Section 24)
- Whoever fails to comply with any of the provisions of the Act, or the rules made, or the directions issued, shall be **punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine up to ₹1 lakh, or both**.
- For continued contravention, an **additional fine of up to ₹5,000 per day** may be imposed.
- The offence is **cognizable, bailable, and non-compoundable** (in the original text; certain amendments expanded scope).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: Power-Section Mapping of EPA, 1986 (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)**

| Section | Power Conferred | KTU Exam Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **S. 3** | Power of Central Govt to take all necessary measures | Very High |
| **S. 5** | Appointment of officers & their powers | High |
| **S. 6** | Power to prescribe emission/discharge standards | Very High |
| **S. 7** | Power to prescribe environmental quality standards | High |
| **S. 7A** | Directions for closure/prohibition of polluting units | Very High |
| **S. 8** | Prohibition/regulation on handling hazardous substances | High |
| **S. 9** | Rule-making power for hazardous substances | Medium |
| **S. 10** | Appointment of Inspectorate | Medium |
| **S. 11** | Power to give directions (including closure) | Very High |
| **S. 12–13** | Recognising laboratories | Low |
| **S. 15** | Penalty for contravention by Department/Company | High |
| **S. 24** | Penalty for non-compliance (₹1 lakh / 5 yrs) | Very High |
| **S. 26** | Effect of other laws — **EPA prevails over other laws** | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2: Rules & Notifications Issued Under EPA, 1986**

| Rule / Notification | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986** | Core implementation framework |
| **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 1989** | Handling & disposal of hazardous waste |
| **Manufacture, Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemicals Rules, 1989** | (MSIHC Rules) — for industrial safety |
| **EIA Notification, 1994** | First mandatory EIA framework |
| **EIA Notification, 2006** | Comprehensive EIA framework |
| **Noise Pollution Rules, 2000** | Ambient noise standards |
| **E-Waste Management Rules, 2016** | E-waste handling |
| **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016** | Plastic waste |
| **Bio-Medical Waste Management Rules, 2016** | Hospital waste |
| **Construction & Demolition Waste Rules, 2016** | C&D waste |
| **EIA Notification, 2020 (Draft)** | Replacement of 2006 notification (under litigation) |

## 2.4 Role of EPA 1986 in EIA Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The **EIA Notification, 2006** (and the 1994 one) is **legally derived from Section 3(2)(v) of the EPA, 1986**, which empowers the Central Government to "lay down standards for the quality of environment in its various aspects." Without the EPA, there would be **no legal basis** for making EIA mandatory.

```
Flow of Legal Authority:
Constitution (Art. 48A, 51A) 
        ↓
Stockholm Declaration (1972) & Bhopal Disaster (1984)
        ↓
EPA, 1986 (Umbrella Act)
        ↓
EIA Notification 1994 / 2006
        ↓
State-Level Environmental Impact Assessment Authorities (SEIAAs)
        ↓
Project-Specific Environmental Clearance
```

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a B.Tech engineer in **Civil, Chemical, Mechanical, or Industrial Engineering**, the EPA 1986 directly affects:

- **Setting up any new industry** — requires Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO) under SPCB, governed by EPA standards.
- **Emission & Effluent Discharge** — limits specified under EPA Rules apply to **stacks and effluent outlets**.
- **Construction Projects** — clearance required under EIA Notification (issued under EPA) for projects above specified thresholds.
- **Hazardous Chemical Storage** — MSIHC Rules 1989 (issued under EPA) require Safety Reports, On-site/Off-site Emergency Plans.
- **Penalties & Liability** — non-compliance can attract closure orders under S. 5(5) and S. 11.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix & Exhaustive Legal Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **legal-regulatory subject**, the "Step-by-Step Derivation" is replaced with an **exhaustive analytical matrix of statutory provisions, their logical structure, and the chain of powers**. Every legal transition is explicitly laid out.

## 3.1 Chain of Statutory Power — Logical Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1: Identification of Environmental Problem**
>
> The Central Government, on receipt of information (or suo motu), identifies that an activity, substance, or process is causing or likely to cause **environmental pollution**. (Source: S. 3(1) EPA, 1986)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 2: Coordination with State Governments**
>
> The Central Government coordinates actions of State Governments, agencies, and statutory authorities. (Source: S. 3(2)(a) EPA, 1986)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 3: Prescription of Standards**
>
> The Central Government prescribes standards for:
> - Quality of environment (ambient air, water bodies) — *S. 7*
> - Maximum allowable limits of emission/discharge — *S. 6*
> - Handling of hazardous substances — *S. 9*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 4: Issuance of Directions**
>
> The Central Government issues specific **directions** to any person, officer, or authority. These directions may include:
> - **Closure of the offending unit** (S. 5)
> - **Stoppage or regulation of supply** of electricity, water, or any other service (S. 5)
> - **Stoppage of any activity, process, or operation** (S. 11)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 5: Appointment of Inspectorate**
>
> An Inspectorate is appointed to monitor compliance and submit reports. (Source: S. 10)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 6: Enforcement & Penalty**
>
> Failure to comply = Imprisonment up to 5 years + Fine up to ₹1 lakh + additional fine up to ₹5,000/day for continued contravention. (Source: S. 24)

## 3.2 Exhaustive Section-Wise Legal Analysis (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3: Detailed Section-Wise Breakdown**

| Section | Title | Power/Duty | Engineering/EIA Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S. 1** | Short Title, Extent & Commencement | This Act may be called the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Extends to whole of India | Foundation clause |
| **S. 2** | Definitions | Defines "environment", "pollutant", "pollution", "hazardous substance", "occupier" | Every term in the Act flows from these definitions |
| **S. 3(1)** | Power of Central Govt to take measures | To protect & improve environment, prevent, control & abate pollution | The **enabling parent provision** — all other powers derive from this |
| **S. 3(2)** | Specific measures | (a) coordination (b) planning (c) execution of programmes (d) prescribing standards (e) collection/dissemination of info (f) inspection (g) research (h) other matters | This is the **powerhouse** — each sub-clause spawns rules & notifications |
| **S. 4** | Appointment of Central & State Boards | Advise on matters referred under S. 12 of Water Act, S. 28 of Air Act — not new boards | Ties EPA into the existing CPCB/SPCB framework |
| **S. 5** | Power to give directions | Includes power to direct closure, prohibition, or regulation of any industry/process | The **strongest operational power** — can shut down polluting units |
| **S. 6** | Rules on standards for emission/discharge | Central Govt may prescribe standards | Basis for industrial emission/effluent norms |
| **S. 7** | Standards for environmental quality | Central Govt may prescribe quality standards | Basis for ambient air/water quality standards |
| **S. 7A** | Special provisions for Delhi (added 1989) | Specific to Union Territory of Delhi (post-incident like Taj Trapezium) | High exam relevance for regional questions |
| **S. 8** | Handling of hazardous substances | Power to prohibit/restrict handling | Foundation for MSIHC Rules 1989 |
| **S. 9** | Rule-making power | Central Govt may make rules on hazardous substance handling | Procedure for industry |
| **S. 10** | Inspectorate | Appointment, powers of inspectors, reporting | On-the-ground enforcement |
| **S. 11** | Power to give directions | To any person, including closure of premises | Comparable to S. 5 — slightly different in scope |
| **S. 12–13** | Recognition of laboratories | Government laboratories empowered to analyse samples | Forensic/legal evidence chain |
| **S. 14** | Report of the analysis | The analysis report of a recognised lab is admissible as evidence | Procedural — important for prosecution |
| **S. 15** | Penalty for contravention by Government Dept. / Company | Where contravention is by a department, the Head is liable; by a company, every person in charge is liable | Vicarious liability |
| **S. 16** | Offences by companies | Specifies persons liable when offence by company: (a) in-charge (b) directors, when offence is with their consent | Vicarious corporate liability |
| **S. 17** | Cognizance of offences | No court shall take cognizance except on complaint by/with sanction of authorised person | Procedural safeguard |
| **S. 18** | Offences to be non-cognizable, bailable etc. | Original Act — but amended by Environment Courts Act, 2010 — offences now cognizable & non-bailable | Updated info — important |
| **S. 19** | Power to make rules | Rule-making by Central Govt | Delegated legislation |
| **S. 20** | Application of Sections 4 & 5 of CrPC to offences | Investigating officer has powers of a police officer | Procedural |
| **S. 22** | Power of court to order closure | Court trying an offence can order closure, if satisfied pollution is being caused | Judicial intervention |
| **S. 24** | Penalty for failure to comply | Imprisonment ≤ 5 years OR fine ≤ ₹1 lakh OR both; + ₹5,000/day for continuing | **Most cited section in exams** |
| **S. 26** | Effect of other laws | Provisions of EPA are in addition to and not in derogation of any other law | Umbrella nature confirmed |

## 3.3 Landmark Judicial Interpretations (KTU-Ready Case Law)

> [!NOTE]
> The Supreme Court of India has used EPA 1986 as the foundation for **judicial environmentalism**. The following cases are **KTU high-yield**:

**Case 1: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case, 1987)**
- The Apex Court read the EPA along with Article 21 (Right to Life) and established the **Absolute Liability Doctrine** — an enterprise engaged in a hazardous activity is absolutely liable for harm caused, irrespective of any fault or negligence.

**Case 2: Indian Council for Enviro-Legal Action v. Union of India (1996)**
- **Oleum Gas Case continuation.** The Court held that where an industry is causing irreparable damage, the principle of **Polluter Pays** mandates that the industry bear the cost of remedial measures.

**Case 3: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996)**
- The Court crystallized the **Precautionary Principle** and the **Polluter Pays Principle** as part of the environmental law of the land, both flowing from EPA 1986.

**Case 4: T.N. Godavarman v. Union of India (Writ Petition 202/1995 — ongoing Forest Conservation)**
- The Court invoked EPA 1986 extensively to regulate forest and biodiversity conservation across India.

## 3.4 Doctrines Established Under EPA, 1986 (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Pillars of Indian Environmental Jurisprudence (sourced largely from EPA 1986 + Constitutional mandates):**

1. **Sustainable Development** — Development that meets present needs without compromising future generations.
2. **Precautionary Principle** — Where there is a threat of serious/irreversible damage, lack of full scientific certainty shall NOT be used as a reason to postpone cost-effective measures.
3. **Polluter Pays Principle** — The polluter is liable to pay for the cost of pollution control and damage.
4. **Public Trust Doctrine** — The State holds natural resources in trust for the public and cannot transfer/divest them to private ownership if it substantially impairs the public interest.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is **legal-regulatory**, the diagrams are designed as **functional architecture flow** schematics to map the legal hierarchy, enforcement chain, and EIA linkage.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Legal Architecture of EPA 1986

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India] --> B[Article 48A - State Duty to Protect Environment]
    A --> C[Article 51A - Fundamental Duty of Citizens]

```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Enforcement & Penalty Chain

```mermaid

    A[Industry or Project] --> B{Are EPA Standards Met?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Operational Compliance]
    B -- No --> D[SPCB CPCB Inspection]

    N --> P[Fine up to 1 lakh]

```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — EPA 1986 as Umbrella Legislation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EPA[Environment Protection Act 1986 - Umbrella]
    EPA --> W[Water Act 1974]
    EPA --> A[Air Act 1981]

    EPA --> NO[Noise Pollution Rules 2000]
    EPA --> EI[EIA Notification 2006]

```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — KTU Module 1 Conceptual Map (Where EPA 1986 Fits)

```mermaid

    M1[Module 1: Introduction to EIA and Environmental Legislation]
    M1 --> T1[Definition and Concept of EIA]
